entries.text: I have finished The Wings of the Dove, and make this comment. His manipulation becomes so elaborate towards the end that instead of feeling the artist you merely feel the man who is posing the subject. And then I think he loses the power to feel the crisis. He becomes merely excessively ingenious. This, you seem to hear him saying, is the way to do it. Now just when you expect a crisis, the true artist evades it. Never do the thing, and it will be all the more impressive. Finally, after all this juggling and arranging of silk pocket handkerchiefs, one ceases to have any feeling for the figure behind. Milly thus manipulated disappears. He overreaches himself. And then one can never read it again. The mental grasp and stretch are magnificent. Not a flabby or slack sentence, but much emasculated by this timidity or consciousness or whatever it is. Very highly American, I conjecture, in the determination to be highly bred, and the slight obtuseness as to what high breeding is.

Roger died on Sunday. Tomorrow we go up, following some instinct, to the funeral. I feel dazed; very wooden. Women cry, L. says: but I don't know why I cry—mostly with Nessa. And I'm too stupid to write anything. My head all stiff. I think the poverty of life now is what comes to me; and this blackish veil over everything. Hot weather; a wind blowing. The substance gone out of everything. I don't think this is exaggerated. It'll come back I suppose. Indeed I feel a great wish, now and then, to live more all over the place, to see people, to create, only for the time one can't make the effort. And I can't write to Helen, but I must now shut this and try.

Maupassant, on writers (true I think). "En lui aucun sentiment simple n'existe plus. Tout ce qu'il voit, ses joies, ses plaisirs, ses souffrances, ses désespoirs, deviennent instantanément des sujets d'observation. Il analyse malgré tout, malgré lui, sans fin, les coeurs, les visages, les gestes, les intonations."

Remember turning aside at mother's bed, when she had died, and Stella * took us in, to laugh, secretly, at the nurse crying. She's pretending, I said, aged 13, and was afraid I was not feeling enough. So now.

The writer's temperament.

"Ne jamais souffrir, penser, aimer, sentir, comme tout le monde, bonnement, franchement, simplement, sans s'analyser soi-même après chaque joie et après chaque sanglot."

Sur l'eau 116

Mornings which are neither quiet nor heavenly, but mixed of hell and ecstasy: never have I had such a hot balloon in my head as re-writing The Years: because it's so long; and the pressure is so terrific. But I will use all my art to keep my head sane. I will stop writing at 11:30 and read Italian or Dryden and so dandle myself along. To Ethel * at Miss Hudson's yesterday. As I sat in the complete English gentleman's home, I wondered how anybody could tolerate that equipage; and thought how a house should be portable like a snail shell. In future perhaps people will flirt out houses like little fans; and go on. There'll be no settled life within walls. There were endless clean, well repaired rooms. A maid in a cap. Cakes on pagoda trays. A terrible array of glossy brown furniture and books—red sham leather. Many nice old rooms, but the manor house has been embellished and made of course self consciously elaborate. A ballroom; a library—empty. And Miss Hudson all brushed up with her Pekinese, a competent ex-mayor of Eastbourne, with waved grey hair; and all so neat and stout; and the silver frames askew; and the air of order, respectability, commonplace. "I'm going to call on the vicar's wife." Ethel immensely red and stout: churning out, poor old woman, the usual indefatigable egotism about deafness and her Mass. She must have a scene every six months. No. But of course, to go deaf, to be 76—well, back to Charleston with Eve and Angelica.

A gale has risen. Weather broken. Armada weather. No sound of planes today, only wind. Terrific air traffic last night. But the raid beaten off by new London barrage. This is cheering. If we can hold out this week—next week—week after—if the weather's turned—if the force of the raids on London is broken—we go up tomorrow to see John about moving Press; to patch the windows, rescue valuables and get letters—if, that is, we're allowed in the Square. Oh, blackberrying I conceived, or re-moulded, an idea for a Common History book—to read from one end of literature including biography; and range at will, consecutively.

In the evening Dr. Löw at our apartment. Another traveler to Palestine. Is taking the bar examination a year before the end of his clerkship and traveling with 1200 K (in 14 days) to Palestine. Would look for a position at the Palestine Office. All these travelers to Palestine (Bergmann, Dr. Kellner) have lowered eyes, feel blinded by their listeners, run their outstretched fingers over the table, their voices crack, they smile weakly and maintain this smile with some irony.—Dr. Kellner said that his students are chauvinists, constantly have the Maccabees in their mouths and want to take after them.

 I realize that I’ve written to Dr. Schiller so readily and well only because Frl. Bauer stayed in Breslau, albeit 14 days ago now, and a scent of her is still in the air, since I previously thought a lot about having flowers sent to her through Dr. Schiller.

 15 (September 1912) Engagement of my sister Valli

 From the depths

 of fatigue

 we rise

 with new powers

 —

 Dark lords

 who wait

 until the children

 exhaust themselves

 Love between brother and sister—the repetition of the love between mother and father

 The presentiment of the sole biographer

 The hollow burned into our surroundings by the work of genius is a good place to put in one’s little light. Hence the incitement that emanates from genius, the general incitement that fires one not only to imitation.

 18 (September 1912) Hubalek’s stories yesterday in the office. The stonebreaker who begged a frog from him on the country road, held it by the feet, and with three bites gobbled down first the little head, then the body and finally the feet.—The best method of killing cats, which have a very tenacious life: one squeezes the neck between a closed door and pulls on the tail.—His aversion to vermin. In the military one night something was itching him under his nose, he reached for it in his sleep and crushed something. But the something was a bug and he carried the stench of it around with him for days.—Four people were eating a finely prepared roast cat, but only three of them knew what they were eating. After the meal the three began to meow, but the fourth refused to believe it, only when they showed him the bloody skin did he believe it, couldn’t run out quickly enough to vomit everything up and was seriously ill for two weeks.—This stonebreaker ate nothing but bread and whatever else of fruit or living things came his way and drank nothing, but brandy. Slept in the brick shed of a brickyard. Once Hubalek met him at twilight in the fields. “Stop” said the man or—Hubalek stopped for the fun of it. “Give me your cigarette” the man went on. Hub. gave it to him. “Give me another!” So you want another? Hub. asked him, held his knotted cane ready in his left hand just in case and with his right gave him a blow in the face so that he dropped the cigarette. The man immediately ran away, cowardly and weak as such schnapps drinkers are.

 Yesterday at Bergmann’s with Dr. Löw. Song about Reb Dovidl, Reb Dovidl, der Wassilkower fährt heint nach Tale. In a city between Wassilko and Tale sung indifferently, in Wassilko tearfully, in Tale joyfully.

 19 (September 1912) Controller Pokorny tells about the trip he took as a 13-year-old boy with 70 Kreuzer in his pocket in the company of a schoolmate. How they arrived in the evening at a tavern where a huge drinking bout was going on, in honor of the mayor who had returned from the military. More than 50 empty beer bottles stood on the floor. Everything was full of pipe smoke. The stench of beer cheese. The two small boys against the wall. The drunken mayor, who in memory of the military wants to establish order everywhere, approaches them and threatens to have them sent home by force as runaways, for which he takes them despite all explanations. The boys tremble, show their Gymnasium identification cards, decline the word mensa, a half-drunken teacher watches without helping. Without getting a clear decision on their fate, they are compelled to join in the drinking, are very pleased to get for nothing so much good beer, which they never could have allowed themselves with their scant means. They drink their fill and then late at night after the last guests have marched off, lie down to sleep on thinly heaped straw in this room that hasn’t been aired and sleep like lords. Except that at 4 o’clock a huge maid comes with a broom, declares she has no time and would have swept them out into the morning mist if they hadn’t run away of their own accord. When the room had been cleaned up a little, two large coffee pots filled to the brim were placed on the table for them. But as they stirred their coffee with the spoon, something big, dark, round kept coming to the surface from time to time. They thought it would be cleared up with time, and drank with relish until, in the face of the half empty pot and the dark thing, they became afraid after all and asked the maid for advice. Then it turned out that the blackness was old congealed goose blood, which had remained in the pots from the previous day’s banquet and over which the coffee had simply been poured in a morning daze. Immediately the boys ran out and vomited everything to the last little drop. Later they were called before the priest, who after a brief examination in religion determined that they were good boys, had the cook serve them soup and then sent them off with his sacred blessing. As pupils of a Gymnasium run by priests they had this soup and this blessing given to them in almost all the parishes through which they passed.

 —

 20 (September 1912) Letters to Löwy and Frl. Taussig yesterday, to Frl. Bauer and Max today.

 —

 It was on a Sunday morning at the height of spring. Georg Bendemann, a young businessman, was sitting in his private room on the second floor of one of the low lightly built houses that extended along the river in a long row differing almost only in height and coloring. He had just finished a letter to a friend of his youth who was now abroad, then sealed it in playful slowness and with his elbow resting on the desk looked out the window at the river, the bridge and the hills on the opposite bank with their pale green. He thought about how this friend, dissatisfied with his progress at home, had positively fled to Russia several years ago. Now he was running a business in St. Petersburg that had begun very promisingly, but for a long time now seemed to be stagnating, as the friend complained on his increasingly rare visits. Thus he toiled away uselessly in a foreign land, the foreign-looking full beard hardly covered the face so familiar from childhood, the yellow complexion of which seemed to indicate a developing illness. By his own account he had no real connection with the colony of his compatriots there, but also barely any social intercourse with native families and was thus preparing himself for a permanent bachelorhood.

 What should one write to such a man, who had obviously lost his way, whom one could pity but not help. Should one perhaps advise him to come home again, transplant his existence here, to resume all his old friendly relations, which certainly nothing hindered him from doing and otherwise rely on the help of friends. But that was tantamount to telling him the more gently the more hurtfully that his efforts up to now had failed, that he should finally abandon them, that he had to return and be gaped at by all as a man who had returned forever, that only his friends understood anything and that he was an old child, who simply had to be guided by his successful companions who had stayed home. And was it certain even then that all the torment one would have to inflict on him would have a purpose. Perhaps it wouldn’t even be possible to bring him home at all, indeed he himself said that he no longer understood the way things worked at home, and so he would then remain nonetheless in his foreign land, embittered by the advice and estranged a bit further still from his friends. But if he really did follow the advice and was then oppressed here, not intentionally, of course, but by the facts, got on well neither with his friends nor without them, suffered from humiliation, now really had no home and no friends, wouldn’t it be much better for him if he stayed in the foreign land as he was. Under such circumstances, could one think that he would actually make headway here.

 For these reasons, if one still wanted to maintain the correspondence at all, one couldn’t send him any actual news such as one wouldn’t hesitate to tell even the most distant acquaintances. The friend had not been home for over three years now and explained this very inadequately by the uncertainty of the political conditions in Russia, which apparently didn’t permit even the briefest absence of a small businessman, while hundreds of thousands of Russians traveled around the world without difficulty. In the course of these three years, however, much had changed for Georg in particular. The death of Georg’s mother, which had occurred about two years ago and since which Georg had lived in a shared household with his old father, had certainly come to his friend’s knowledge, and he had expressed his sympathy in a letter with a dryness that could have stemmed only from the fact that the grief over such an event becomes completely unimaginable in a foreign land. But since that time Georg had tackled his business as well as everything else with greater determination. Perhaps while his mother was alive his father, by refusing to accept any view but his own in the business, had hindered him from taking any real initiative, perhaps since his mother’s death his father although he still worked in the business, had grown more restrained, perhaps—it was even very probable—fortunate accidents played a far more important role—but in any case the business had developed quite unexpectedly in these two years, they had had to double the staff, the turnover had quintupled, further progress undoubtedly lay ahead.

 The friend, however, had no inkling of this change. In the past, perhaps for the last time in that letter of sympathy, he had tried to persuade Georg to emigrate to Russia and had enlarged on the prospects that existed in St. Petersburg for Georg’s line of business in particular. The figures were infinitesimal compared to the scale Georg’s business had now assumed. But Georg hadn’t felt inclined to write to his friend about his business successes and to do so belatedly now would have really looked odd.

 So Georg confined himself to writing to his friend always only about insignificant events such as heap themselves up in one’s memory when one is reflecting on a quiet Sunday. All he wanted was to leave undisturbed the image that his friend must have formed of his hometown in the long interval and to which he had also reconciled himself. So it happened to Georg that he announced to his friend the engagement of an inconsequential person to an equally inconsequential girl three times in letters written rather far apart, until quite contrary to Georg’s intention his friend actually began to take an interest in this oddity.

 But Georg much preferred to write to him about such things than to confess that he himself had become engaged a month ago to a Fräulein Frieda Brandenfeld a girl from a well-to-do family. Often he spoke with his bride about this friend and the special correspondence relationship he had with him. Then he definitely won’t be coming to our wedding she said and yet I have the right to meet all your friends. “I don’t want to bother him replied Georg, don’t misunderstand me, he would probably come, at least I think so, but he would feel forced and aggrieved, would perhaps envy me and surely dissatisfied and unable to rid himself of the dissatisfaction would go back again alone. Alone—do you know what that is.” “Yes but can’t he find out about our marriage in another way.” “I can’t prevent that, certainly, but with his way of life it’s unlikely.” “But really if you have friends like that, Georg you shouldn’t have married at all.” “Well both of us are to blame for that, but I wouldn’t want it any other way.” And when, breathing rapidly under his kisses, she still asserted “Actually it does hurt me” he thought it really was harmless to write everything to his friend. This is the way I am and he has to take me as I am he said to himself. I can’t cut a person out of myself who might be more suited to friendship with him than I am.

 And in the long letter that he wrote on this Sunday morning he did indeed inform his friend of the engagement that had taken place with the following words: “The best news I have saved for the end. I’ve become engaged to a Fräulein Frieda Brandenhof a girl from a well-to-do family who settled here only long after your departure whom you could thus hardly know. There will be opportunity later to tell you more about my bride, today let it be enough for you that I’m very happy and that in our mutual relationship something has changed only to the extent that you will now have in me instead of an entirely ordinary friend a happy one. In addition you get in my bride, who sends her warm regards and will soon write to you herself, a genuine fem. friend which for a bachel. is not entirely without significance. I know all sorts of things are holding you back from visiting us, but wouldn’t my wedding be just the right occasion to cast aside all obstacles? But be that as it may, act without any regard and only as you think best.”

 With this letter in his hand G. had been sitting for a long time at his desk his face turned toward the window. To an acquaintance passing by and greeting him from the street he had barely responded with an absent smile.

 Finally he put the letter in his pocket and went out of his room across a small corridor into his father’s room, which he had not entered for months now. Nor was there generally any need to do so, for he had constant dealings with his father in the business, they had lunch at the same time in an eating house, at dinner, it was true, each of them provided for himself as he pleased, but usually, unless Georg as happened most often was together with friends or now visiting his bride, they sat for a little while in the evening each with his newspaper in the shared living room.

 Georg was astonished at how dark his father’s room was even on this sunny morning. So the high wall that rose on the other side of the narrow courtyard cast such a shadow. His father was sitting by the window in a corner decorated with various mementos of his late mother, and was reading the newspaper, which he held to the side in front of his eyes in an attempt to make up for some weakness of vision. On the table were the remains of his breakfast, not much of which seemed to have been consumed. Ah Georg said his father, immediately going to meet him. His heavy dressing gown opened a little as he walked, the ends fluttered around him, my father is still a giant Georg said to himself. It’s unbearably dark in here he then said. Yes it is dark replied his father. You’ve closed the window too?

 I prefer it like this.

 It’s very warm outside said Georg as if in an addendum to his previous remark and sat down.

 His father cleared away the breakfast dishes and put them on a cabinet.

 I actually only wanted to tell you Georg went on, following the old man’s movements in great perplexity, that I’ve now sent word of my engagement to St. Petersburg after all. He pulled the letter out of his pocket a little and let it fall back in again.

 Why to St. Petersburg? asked his father.

 To my friend of course said Georg, seeking his father’s eyes. He’s really completely different in the business he thought. The way he sits here spread out and crosses his arms over his chest.

 Yes.—To your friend. his father said with emphasis.

 You know Father, that I at first wanted to keep my engagement from him. Out of consideration, for no other reason. You yourself know, he’s a difficult person. I said to myself, he might hear about my engagement from somewhere else, even if that’s hardly likely with his solitary way of life—that I can’t prevent—but from me myself he is simply not going to hear about it.

 And now you’ve changed your mind? asked his father, laying the large newspaper on the windowsill and on the newspaper his glasses, which he covered with his hand.

 Yes now I’ve changed my mind. If he is my good friend I said to myself then my happy engagement will make him happy too. And so I have no longer hesitated to inform him of it. But before I mailed the letter, I wanted to tell you.

 Georg said his father, stretching his toothless mouth wide listen. You’ve come to me about this matter to consult me. That does you credit no doubt. But it’s nothing, it’s worse than nothing, if you don’t tell me the whole truth now. I don’t want to stir things up that aren’t pertinent. Since the death of our dear mother certain unpleasant things have occurred. Perhaps the time is coming for them too and perhaps it’s coming sooner than we think. In the business much escapes me, perhaps it’s not concealed from me—I don’t at all want to make the assumption now that it’s concealed from me—I’m no longer strong enough, my memory is deteriorating, I can no longer keep track of all the many things. That is first of all the course of nature and secondly the death of our sweet mother has cast me down far more than you.—But because we’re keeping to this matter at the moment, to this letter, I beg you Georg don’t deceive me. It’s a trifle, it’s not worth the breath, so don’t deceive me. Do you really have this friend in St. Petersburg?

 Georg stood up in embarrassment. Never mind my friends. A thousand friends can’t replace my father for me. Do you know what I think? You’re not looking after yourself enough. But age demands its due. You’re indispensable to me in the business, you know that very well, but if the business should threaten your health, I would close it tomorrow forever. This won’t do. We have to introduce a new way of life for you. And I mean thoroughly. You sit here in the dark and in the living room you’d have beautiful light. You nibble at your breakfast instead of strengthening yourself properly. You sit by a closed window and the air would do you such good. No my father. I will fetch the doctor and we will follow his orders. We will exchange rooms, you will move into the front room, I here. It will be no change for you, everything will be brought over with you. But there’s time for all that, now lie down a little in bed, you really need rest. Come I’ll help you get undressed, you’ll see, I can do it. Or do you want to go straight to the front room, then you can lie down in my bed for the time being. That would actually be very sensible.

 Georg stood just next to his father, who had let his head with its shaggy white hair sink to his chest.

 “Georg” his father said softly without moving.

 Georg immediately knelt down beside his father, he saw the pupils in his father’s weary face, overlarge in the corners of his eyes, directed at him.

 You have no friend in St. Petersburg. You’ve always been a joker and unfortunately haven’t held yourself back even with me. How could you have a friend there of all places. I simply can’t believe it.

 “Just think Father” said Georg, lifting his father from the chair and as he stood there now quite weakly, taking off his dressing gown. Now it will soon be three years ago that my friend visited us. I still remember that you were not especially fond of him. At least twice I denied to you that he was there even though he was sitting in my room. I could actually understand your aversion to him quite well, my friend has his peculiarities. But then you had a really good talk with him after all. I was so proud at the time that you listened to him, nodded and asked questions. If you think, you’re bound to remember. That was when he told incredible stories of the Russian Revolution. How, for example, on a business trip in Kiev during a riot he had seen an Armenian priest on a balcony who cut a broad blood cross into the palm of his hand, raised this hand and appealed to the crowd. You yourself have even retold this story now and then.

 Meanwhile Georg had managed to sit his father down again and to carefully take off the knitted pants he wore over his white underpants, as well as his socks. At the sight of the not particularly clean underwear, he reproached himself for having neglected his father. It would certainly have been his duty to watch over the changing of his father’s underwear. He had not yet spoken explicitly with his bride about how they wanted to arrange his father’s future, for they had tacitly presumed that his father would remain alone in the old apartment. But now he abruptly resolved with all firmness to take his father with him into his future household. On closer inspection it almost seemed that the care that would be given to his father there might come too late.

 In his arms he carried his father to the bed. He had a terrible feeling when he noticed during the few steps to the bed that his father was playing with his watch chain. He couldn’t lay him in the bed right away, so firmly did he cling to this watch chain

 But no sooner was he in the bed than everything seemed good. He covered himself up and then pulled the blanket especially high over his shoulder. He looked up at Georg in a not unfriendly way.

 You’re beginning to remember him aren’t you asked Georg, nodding encouragingly to him.

 “Am I well covered now” asked his father as if he couldn’t see whether his feet were sufficiently covered.

 So you already like being in bed said Georg, tucking the covers better around him

 Am I well covered up his father asked again and seemed to be waiting especially intently for the answer

 Just rest peacefully, you’re well covered up.

 No his father cried so that the answer collided with the question, threw the cover back with such strength that for a moment it completely unfolded in mid-air, and stood upright in the bed, he held only one hand lightly against the ceiling. “You wanted to cover me up, I know that my little scamp but I’m not covered up yet. And even if it’s my last strength, enough for you, too much for you. Certainly I know your friend. He would have been a son after my own heart. And that’s why you’ve been deceiving him all these years. Why else? Do you think I haven’t wept for him? That’s why you lock yourself in your office, no one is to disturb, the boss is busy just so that you can write your false little letters to Russia. But fortunately no one has to teach the father to see through the son. When you thought you’d brought him down brought him so far down that you could sit on him with your behind and he wouldn’t budge, that’s when my fine son decided to get married.

 Georg looked up at the horrible vision of his father. The St. Petersburg friend, whom his father suddenly knew so well, moved him as never before. He saw him lost in vast Russia. He saw him at the door of the empty plundered shop. He barely remained standing among the wreckage of the shelves, the shredded wares, the falling gas fixtures. Why had he had to go so far away.

 But look at me his father cried and Georg ran almost distractedly to the bed to take everything in but came to a stop halfway.

 Because she lifted her skirts his father began to flute because she lifted her skirts like this, the disgusting goose, and to portray it he lifted his shirt so high that one could see the scar from his war years on his thigh because she lifted her skirts like this and like this and like this, you made advances to her and so that you can take your pleasure with her undisturbed, you have desecrated our mother’s memory, betrayed your friend and stuck your father in bed so that he can’t move. But can he move or not?

 And he stood completely free and kicked his legs. He was radiant with insight.

 Georg stood in a corner, as far as possible from his father. A long while ago he resolved firmly to observe everything with complete precision so that he couldn’t somehow be caught indirectly, from behind, from above. Now he remembered the long-forgotten resolution again and forgot it, as one pulls a short thread through the eye of a needle.

 But now your friend isn’t betrayed after all his father cried and his wagging index finger reinforced it. I was his local representative here.

 “Playactor!” Georg couldn’t help crying, immediately realized the damage done and with staring eyes he bit his tongue only too late, so that he doubled over in pain.

 Yes indeed I’ve been playacting. Playacting, good word. What other comfort remained for the old widowed father. Tell me—and for the moment of the answer still be my living son—what remained for me in my back room, plagued by a disloyal staff old to the marrow of my bones. And my son went through the world in jubilation, closed business deals that I had prepared, tumbled over himself with pleasure and walked away from his father with the reserved face of a man of honor. Do you think I didn’t love you, I, from whom you came.

 Now he will bend forward thought Georg. If he fell and shattered! These words hissed through his head.

 His father bent forward, but didn’t fall. Since Georg didn’t approach, as he had expected, he straightened up again.

 Stay where you are, I don’t need you. You think you still have the strength to come here and are merely holding yourself back because you want to. Don’t be mistaken. I’m still the much stronger one. Alone I might have had to retreat, but as it is your mother has given me her strength, with your friend I’ve formed a splendid alliance, your customers I have here in my pocket.

 Even in his shirt he has pockets Georg said to himself, and believed that with this remark he could discredit him with the whole world. Only for a moment did he think so for he kept forgetting everything.

 Just link arms with your bride and come toward me. I’ll sweep her from your side, you don’t know how.

 Georg made grimaces, as if he didn’t believe it. His father merely nodded affirming the truth of what he said, toward Georg’s corner.

 How you amused me today when you came and asked whether you should write to your friend about the engagement. He knows everything, stupid boy, he knows everything. I write to him, because you forgot to take my writing things away from me. That’s why he hasn’t come for years now, he knows everything a hundred times better than you yourself do. He crumples your letters unread in his left hand while in his right he holds up my letters to read.

 In his enthusiasm he swung his arm over his head.

 He knows everything a thousand times better cried his father.

 Ten thousand times said Georg, to ridicule his father, but while still in his mouth the words took on a deadly serious tone.

 “For years I’ve been waiting for you to come with this question. Do you think I care about anything else, do you think I read newspapers. Here!” And he threw him a newspaper sheet that had somehow been carried with him into the bed. An old newspaper with a name completely unknown to Georg.

 How long you hesitated before you reached maturity. Your mother had to die, she couldn’t live to see the joyful day, your friend is going to ruin in his Russia, 3 years ago he was already yellow enough to be thrown away, and I, well, you see how I’m doing. For that you have eyes.

 So you’ve been lying in wait for me! cried Georg.

 Pityingly his father said in passing: You probably wanted to say that earlier. It isn’t even appropriate anymore

 And louder: So now you know what else there was besides you, until now you knew only of yourself! You were truly an innocent child, but even more truly you were a devilish human being!

 “And therefore know, I sentence you now to death by drowning!”

 Georg felt himself cast out of the room, the crash with which his father collapsed behind him onto the bed he still carried away in his ears. On the staircase, hurrying down the steps as if down a flat slope, he took his cleaning woman by surprise on her way up to tidy the apartment after the night. “Jesus!” she cried, covering her face with her apron, but he was already gone. He leaped out the front door, was driven across the road to the water. Already he clung to the railing like a hungry man to food. He swung himself over, like the outstanding gymnast he had been in his youthful years to his parents’ pride. Still he held on with weakening hands, spied through the bars of the railing an auto-omnibus, which would easily muffle the sound of his fall, cried softly, “dear parents I did always love you” and let himself fall.

 At that moment a positively endless stream of traffic was going over the bridge.

 23 (September 1912) This story “The Judgment” I wrote at one stretch on the night of the 22 to the 23 from 10 o’clock in the evening until 6 o’clock in the morning. My legs had grown so stiff from sitting that I could hardly pull them out from under the desk. The terrible strain and joy, how the story unfolded itself before me how I moved forward in an expanse of water. Several times last night I bore my weight on my back. How everything can be risked, how for all, for the strangest ideas a great fire is prepared, in which they die away and rise again. How it turned blue outside the window. A carriage passed. Two men walked across the bridge. At 2 o’clock I looked for the last time at the clock. As the maid walked through the hall for the first time, I wrote down the last sentence. Putting out the lamp and daylight. The slight heart pains. The tiredness passing away in the middle of the night. The trembling entrance into my sisters’ room. Reading aloud. Beforehand stretching in front of the maid and saying: “I’ve been writing until now.” The appearance of the untouched bed, as if it had just been carried in. The confirmed conviction that with my novel writing I am in disgraceful lowlands of writing. Only in this way can writing be done, only with such cohesion, with such complete opening of the body and the soul. Morning in bed. The always clear eyes. Many feelings carried along during the writing: for example, the joy that I will have something beautiful for Max’s Arcadia, thoughts of Freud naturally, at one passage of Arnold Beer, at another of Wassermann, at one (shatter) of Werfel’s Riesin, naturally also of my “The Urban World”

 I, only I am the observer of the ground floor.

 Gustav Blenkelt was a simple man with regular habits. He didn’t like any extravagance and was certain in his judgment toward people who indulged in such extravagance. Although he was a bachelor, he felt himself entirely justified in having a decisive say in marital affairs of his acquaintances, and anyone who even cast doubt on such a justification would have fared badly with him. He was wont to express his opinion openly and never held back listeners whom his opinion didn’t suit at the moment. As everywhere there were people who admired him, people who respected him, people who tolerated him and finally those who wanted nothing to do with him. Indeed every person even the most negligible if one only takes a proper look forms the center of a circle that is twisted together here and there, how could it have been different in the case of Gustav Blenkelt at bottom an especially sociable person?

 In his 35th year the last year of his life he associated especially frequently with a young married couple named Strong. It’s certain that for Herr Strong, who had just opened a furniture store with his wife’s money, being acquainted with Blenkelt had various advantages, since the latter had the bulk of his acquaintances among young marriageable people, who sooner or later had to think about acquiring new furniture for themselves and who out of sheer habit generally didn’t neglect Blenkelt’s advice in this respect either. I keep them on tight reins Blenkelt was wont to say.

 24 (September 1912) My sister said: The apartment (in the story) is very similar to ours. I said: how so? Then the father would have to be living in the water closet.

 25 (September 1912) Restrained myself by force from writing. Rolled in bed. The congestion of blood in the head and the useless flowing by. What harmfulness!—Yesterday read at Baum’s, to the Baums, my sisters, Marta, Frau Dr. Bloch with 2 sons (one of them a one-year volunteer). Toward the end my hand moved around uncontrollably and genuinely before my face. I had tears in my eyes. The indubitableness of the story was confirmed.—This evening tore myself away from writing. Cinematograph at the National Theater. Box. Frl. Oplatka, whom a priest once pursued. She came home soaked in a cold sweat. Danzig. Körner’s life. The horses. The white horse. The powder smoke. Lützow’s Wild Hunt.

 As the 17-year-old Karl Rossmann, who had been sent by his poor parents to America because a maid had seduced him and had a child by him, entered the harbor of Newyork on the now slow-moving ship, he saw the long-observed statue of the goddess of liberty as if in a suddenly more intense sunlight. Her arm with the sword rose as if newly and around her figure blew the free winds.

 “So high” he said to himself and, not thinking at all of leaving, was gradually pushed to the railing by the constantly swelling crowd of porters moving past him.

 A young man with whom he had become casually acquainted on the voyage said as he passed: Well don’t you feel like getting off yet? “Oh yes, I’m ready” said Karl, laughing at him, and in his exuberance and because he was a strong boy, lifted his suitcase onto his shoulder. But glancing over his acquaintance, who, swinging his cane a little, was now moving away with the others, he realized that he had forgotten his umbrella down in the ship. He quickly asked his acquaintance, who didn’t seem very pleased, to be so kind as to wait for a moment with his suitcase, quickly surveyed his surroundings in order to find his bearings on his return and hurried away. Down below, to his dismay, he found a passage that would have considerably shortened his way barred for the first time, which probably had to do with all the passengers disembarking, and he had to find his way laboriously through countless little rooms, perpetually branching corridors, short flights of steps, which, however, kept following each other an empty room with an abandoned desk, until, since he had gone this way only once or twice and always in the company of others, he was indeed utterly lost. In his perplexity and having met no one and heard only the constant scraping of the thousand human feet over him and noticed from a distance like a breath the last workings of the now shut-off engine, he began without thinking to bang on a little door at which he happened to stop in his wandering. “It’s open” called a voice from inside and with a genuine sigh of relief Karl opened the door. “Why are you banging so madly on the door?” asked a huge man, barely looking at Karl. Through some skylight hatch a dim light, long since expended up above on the ship, fell into the pitiful cabin, in which a bed, a wardrobe, a chair and the man stood closely side by side as if stored there. “I’m lost” said Karl “I never quite realized it during the voyage but it’s a terribly big ship.” “Yes you’re right about that” said the man with some pride and didn’t stop fiddling with the lock of a small suitcase, which he kept pressing shut with both hands in order to listen to the bolt snapping into place. “But do come in” the man went on “you’re not just going to stand outside.” Am I not disturbing you, asked Karl. “Oh how could you disturb me.” “Are you a German?” Karl sought to reassure himself, for he had heard a lot about the dangers that threaten newcomers in America, especially from Irishmen. “I am, I am” said the man. Karl still hesitated. Then the man unexpectedly grabbed the door handle, and with the door, which he quickly closed, pushed Karl in with him. “I can’t stand it when people look in at me from the corridor” said the man, who was again working on his suitcase. “Everyone runs by here and looks in, I’d like to see anyone bear it.” “But the corridor is completely empty” said Karl, who stood there squeezed uncomfortably against the bedpost. “Yes, now” said the man. “But we’re talking about now” thought Georg “it’s hard to speak with this man.” “Why don’t you lie down on the bed, you’ll have more room there,” said the man. Karl crawled in as well as he could, laughing out loud at his first unsuccessful attempt to swing himself over. But no sooner was he in there than he cried “For God’s sake, I’ve forgotten all about my suitcase.” “Where is it, then?” “Up on the deck, an acquaintance is keeping an eye on it. What’s his name again?” And from a secret pocket his mother had attached to the lining of his coat for the voyage he pulled a visiting card. Butterbaum, Franz Butterbaum. “Do you really need the suitcase?” “Of course.” “Well then why did you give it to a stranger?” “I’d forgotten my umbrella down below and ran to get it, but didn’t want to drag along the suitcase. And then I got lost.” “You’re alone? Unaccompanied.” “Yes, alone.” Perhaps I should stick with this man, it occurred to Karl, where am I going to find a better friend anytime soon. “And now you’ve lost the suitcase too. Not to mention the umbrella” and the man sat down on the chair as if Karl’s affair had now begun to interest him somewhat. “But I believe the suitcase is not yet lost.” “Blessed are those who believe” said the man, vigorously scratching his dark short thick hair. On the ship the morals change with the ports, in Hamburg your Butterbaum might have watched the suitcase, here there’s most likely not a trace left of either of them. “But then I must go up and see immediately” said Karl, looking around for a way out. “Just stay” said the man and with one hand against his chest pushed him downright roughly back into the bed. But why Karl asked with annoyance. Because there’s no sense in it said the man. In a little while I’ll be leaving too, then we’ll leave together. Either the suitcase has been stolen, then there’s nothing to be done and you can mourn it to the end of your days or the man is still watching it, then he’s an idiot and should go on watching or he’s just an honest man and has left the suitcase where it was then we’ll find it all the more easily once the ship is completely emptied out. And the same goes for your umbrella. Do you know your way around the ship? Karl asked mistrustfully and it seemed to him as if there were a hidden hitch in the otherwise convincing idea that his things would be easiest to find on the empty ship. I am a ship’s stoker after all said the man. You’re a ship’s stoker Karl cried joyfully, as if this exceeded all expectations, and propped up on his elbow took a closer look at the man. “Just outside the small room where I slept with the Slovaks there was a hatch in the wall through which one could see into the engine room.” “Yes, that’s where I’ve been working” said the stoker. “I’ve always been so interested in technology” said Karl, pursuing a particular train of thought “and I would certainly have become an engineer later on if I hadn’t had to leave for America.” “Why did you have to leave, then?” “Oh, never mind that!” said Karl, casting the whole story away with his hand. At the same time he looked at the stoker with a smile, as if begging his indulgence even for what he hadn’t confessed. There must have been a reason said the stoker and one wasn’t quite sure whether he meant by this to demand or fend off the telling of this reason. “Now I could become a stoker too” said Karl “my parents don’t care at all now what I become.” “My job will be available” said the stoker, and in full consciousness of that fact he put his hands in his pants pockets and flung his legs, which were clad in wrinkled, leather-like, iron-gray pants, onto the bed to stretch them. Karl had to move closer to the wall. “You’re leaving the ship?” “That’s right, we’re marching off today.” “But why? Don’t you like it?” Well, it’s just the way things are, it’s not always a matter of whether one likes it or not. Besides you’re right I don’t like it either. You probably aren’t resolved to become a stoker, but that’s just when it’s easiest to become one. So I strongly advise you against it. If you wanted to study in Europe, then why don’t you want to do the same here. After all, the American universities are incomparably better.” “That’s certainly possible, said Karl, but I have barely any money for studying. I did read about someone who worked in a shop during the day and studied at night until he became a doctor and I believe mayor. But that takes great perseverance, doesn’t it? I’m afraid I lack that. Besides I was never an especially good student, leaving school really wasn’t hard for me. And the schools here might be even stricter. I hardly know any English at all. On the whole people here are so prejudiced against foreigners, I believe.” “Have you found that out already too? Well, then it’s all right. Then you’re my man. You see, we’re on a German ship, it belongs to the Hamburg America line, why aren’t we all Germans here? Why is the chief engineer a Romanian? His name is Schubal. It’s incredible. And that dirty dog slave-drives us Germans on a German ship. Don’t think—he ran out of breath, he made a fluttering movement with his hand—that I’m complaining for the sake of complaining. I know that you have no influence and are a poor boy yourself. But it’s too awful.” And he pounded on the table several times with his fist, not taking his eye off it as he pounded. I have served on so many ships—and he mentioned 20 names one after another as if it were one word, Karl grew quite confused—and have distinguished myself, have been praised, was a worker to the taste of my captains, I was even on the same merchant sailing vessel for several years he rose as if that had been the high point of his life—and here on this tub, where everything is done according to the rules, where no wit is required—here I’m good for nothing, here I’m always in Schubal’s way, I’m a slacker deserve to be thrown out and am given my pay out of mercy. Do you understand that? I don’t.” “You mustn’t put up with that” Karl said excitedly. He had almost lost the sense that he was on the unsteady ground of a ship on the coast of an unknown continent, so at home did he feel here on the stoker’s bed. “Have you been to the captain yet? Have you appealed to him for your rights?” “Oh go away, just go. I don’t want you here. You don’t listen to what I say and then give me advice. How am I supposed to go to the captain.” And wearily the stoker sat down again and buried his face in both hands. “There’s no better advice I can give him” Karl said to himself. And on the whole it seemed to him that he should have fetched his suitcase rather than give advice here that was only considered stupid. When his father had handed the suitcase over to him forever, he had asked in jest: How long will you have it? and now that expensive suitcase was perhaps already lost in earnest. The only remaining consolation was that his father couldn’t find out the least thing about his present situation, even if he were to make inquiries. All the shipping company could say was that he had reached Newyork. But Karl was sorry that he had hardly used the belongings in the suitcase yet, even though, for example, he would have needed to change his shirt a long time ago. So he had economized in the wrong place; now just at the beginning of his career when he would need to present himself cleanly dressed, he would have to appear in a dirty shirt. Those were beautiful prospects. Otherwise, the loss of the suitcase wouldn’t have been so bad, for the suit he had on was even better than the one in the suitcase, which was actually only an emergency suit, which his mother had had to mend just before his departure. Now he also remembered that there was still a piece of Veronese salami in the suitcase, which his mother had packed for him as a special treat but of which he had been able to eat only the smallest portion, since he’d had no appetite at all during the voyage and the soup distributed in the steerage had been plenty for him. But now he would have liked to have the sausage at hand to offer as a gift to the stoker. For such people are easily won over when one slips them some trifle, Karl knew that from his father, who won over all the lower employees with whom he had business dealings by distributing cigars. All that Karl had left to give away now was his money, and if he had perhaps already lost his suitcase, he didn’t want to touch that for the time being. Again his thoughts returned to the suitcase and now he really couldn’t understand why he had kept such close watch over the suitcase during the voyage that the watch had almost cost him his sleep, if he had now let this same suitcase be taken away from him so easily. He remembered the five nights during which he had incessantly suspected a little Slovak, who slept two places to his left, of being after his suitcase. This Slovak had just been waiting for Karl finally overcome by weakness to doze off for a moment so that he could use a long rod, with which he was always playing or practicing during the day, to pull the suitcase over to him. By day this Slovak looked innocent enough, but no sooner had night come than he rose every so often from his bed and looked sadly over at Karl’s suitcase. Karl could make this out quite clearly, for there was always someone here or there who with the restlessness of the emigrant had lit a small light, even though this was prohibited by the ship’s regulations and was trying to decipher the incomprehensible brochures of the emigration agencies. If such a light was nearby, then Karl could doze a little, but if it was far away or if the room was dark, then he had to keep his eyes open. The strain of this had really exhausted him. And now it had perhaps been completely in vain. That Butterbaum, if he should ever meet him somewhere.

 At that moment the silence outside, until then complete, was broken by the distant sound of short little taps like the patter of children’s feet, they grew louder as they approached and now it was a steady march of men. They were evidently walking in a line, as was natural in the narrow corridor, one could hear clanking like that of weapons. Karl who had been on the verge of stretching out in the bed for a sleep freed of all worries about suitcases and Slovaks, gave a start and nudged the stoker in order to draw his attention at last, for the head of the procession seemed to have just reached the door. “That’s the ship’s band said the stoker. They were playing up above and are going to pack up. Now everything’s finished and we can go. Come on.” He seized Karl by the hand, took at the last moment a picture of the Mother of God from the wall over the bed, stuffed it into his breast pocket, grabbed his suitcase and hurriedly left the cabin with Karl.

 Now I’m going to the office and will give the gentlemen a piece of my mind. There’s no one left here, one needn’t show any consideration the stoker repeated in various ways, and as he walked he kicked sideways, trying to crush underfoot a rat crossing his path, but merely driving it more quickly into the hole, which it had reached just in time. On the whole he was slow in his movements, for even though he had long legs, they were too heavy.

 They passed through a section of the kitchen where some girls in dirty aprons—they splashed them intentionally—were washing dishes in large tubs. The stoker called a certain Line over to him put his arm around her hip and, while she kept pressing herself coquettishly against his arm, led her along for a short stretch. “It’s time to get paid now, do you want to come along?” he asked. “Why should I bother just bring me the money.” she replied, slipped out from under his arm and ran away. “Where did you pick up the beautiful boy” she called, but didn’t wait for an answer. One could hear the laughter of all the girls, who had stopped their work.

 But they walked on and came to a door, at the top of which was a small front gable borne by small gilded caryatids. For a ship’s fixture it looked rather extravagant. Karl realized he had never entered this area, which had probably been reserved for the first- and second-class passengers during the voyage, whereas now before the great cleaning of the ship the dividing doors had been taken off their hinges. Indeed they had already encountered some men carrying brooms on their shoulders, who had greeted the stoker. Karl marveled at the great bustle, in his steerage he had certainly witnessed little of this. Wires of electric lines also ran along the corridors, and one constantly heard a small bell.

 The stoker knocked respectfully on the door and, when someone shouted come in, gestured to Karl with his hand to enter without fear. He did enter, but remained standing at the door. Outside the room’s three windows he saw the waves of the sea and as he contemplated their cheerful movement his heart pounded as if he had not been looking uninterruptedly at the sea for five long days. Great ships crossed each other’s paths and yielded to the rising and falling waves only as far as their heaviness permitted. If one narrowed one’s eyes, these ships seemed to be rocking from sheer heaviness. On their masts they bore narrow but long flags that, though tautened by the ships’ motion, still wriggled back and forth. Gun salutes rang out, probably from warships, one such ship passed not too far away and its cannon barrels radiant with the reflection from their steel casing were as if caressed by the ship’s sure, smooth and yet not horizontal motion. At least from the door one could observe the small ships and boats only in the distance, entering in droves the gaps between the large ships. But behind all this stood Newyort, looking at Karl with the hundred thousand windows of its skyscrapers. Yes in this room one knew where one was.

 At a round table sat 3 gentlemen, one of them a ship’s officer in a blue ship’s uniform, the other two, officials of the harbor authority, in black American uniforms. Piled high on the table were various documents, which the officer first skimmed with pen in hand, then passed to the other two, who now read them, now made excerpts, now put them in their briefcases, unless one of them, almost uninterruptedly making little noises with his teeth, dictated something for his colleague to take down.

 At a desk by the window, his back to the door sat a smaller gentleman who was busy with large folios that were lined up in front of him on a sturdy bookshelf at head level. Next to him stood an open cash box, which at least at first glance appeared empty.

 The second window was empty and offered the best view. Near the third, however, stood two gentlemen conversing in low voices. One of them was leaning beside the window, also wearing the ship’s uniform and playing with the handle of his sword. The man with whom he spoke was facing the window, and now and then one of his movements exposed part of the row of decorations on the other’s chest. He was in civilian clothes and had a thin bamboo cane, which, since he had set both hands firmly on his hips, also stuck out like a sword.

 Karl didn’t have much time to look at everything, for soon a servant approached them and asked the stoker, giving him a look as if he didn’t belong here, what it was he wanted. The stoker answered as softly as he had been asked that he wanted to speak with the chief purser. The servant for his part rejected this request with a hand movement, but nonetheless walked on tiptoe giving the round table a wide berth to the gentleman with the folios. This gentleman, one could see it clearly, positively stiffened at the servant’s words, but finally turned around to face the man who wished to speak with him, then waved in a sternly dismissive manner at the stoker and for safety’s sake at the servant too. Thereupon the servant returned to the stoker and said in a tone as if he were confiding something to him: “Get out of the room at once!”

 After this response the stoker looked down at Karl, as if the latter were his heart to which he was silently pouring out his sorrow. Without further reflection Karl broke free, ran straight across the room, even lightly brushing against the officer’s chair, the servant ran stooped with his arms prepared to clasp, as if he were chasing vermin, but Karl was the first to reach the chief purser’s desk, to which he clung in case the servant should try to pull him away.

 Naturally the whole room immediately came to life. The ship’s officer at the table had jumped up, the gentlemen from the harbor authority watched calmly but intently, the two gentlemen by the window now stood abreast, the servant, believing he was out of place where his masters themselves showed an interest, stepped back. The stoker at the door waited tensely for the moment when his help would be needed. The chief purser finally made a great turn to the right in his armchair.

 Karl rummaged in his secret pocket, which he had no qualms about revealing to these people’s eyes, and pulled out his passport, which in lieu of further introduction he laid down open on the desk. The chief purser seemed to consider this passport irrelevant, for he flicked it aside with two fingers, whereupon Karl, as if this formality had been satisfactorily completed, put the passport back in his pocket. “I take the liberty of saying, he then began, that in my opinion injustice has been done to the Herr Stoker. There is here a certain Schubal who is giving him trouble. He himself has served to complete satisfaction on many ships, all of which he can name for you, is industrious, applies himself to his work and it’s really incomprehensible why on this ship in particular, where the duties aren’t as excessively onerous as, for example, on merchant sailing vessels, he should be inadequate. It can therefore only be slander that hinders him in his advancement and deprives him of the recognition he would otherwise most certainly not be denied. I’ve spoken only in general terms about this matter, his specific complaints he will present to you himself.” Karl had addressed all the gentlemen with this matter because indeed all were listening, and it seemed far more likely that among all of them together a just man would be found than that this just man should be the chief purser in particular. Out of shrewdness Karl had also concealed the fact that he had known the stoker for only such a short time. Moreover, he would have spoken far better still if he hadn’t been disconcerted by the red face of the gentleman with the bamboo cane, whom he saw for the very first time from where he was now standing.

 “Every word of it is correct” said the stoker, before anyone had even asked him, indeed before they had so much as looked at him. This overhastiness on the stoker’s part would have been a big mistake, if the gentleman with the decorations, who as it now dawned on Karl was definitely the captain, had not apparently already made up his mind to listen to the stoker. For he held out his hand and called to the stoker: Come here! in a voice firm enough to strike with a hammer. Now everything depended on the stoker’s behavior, for as far as the justice of his cause was concerned, Karl had no doubt.

 Fortunately it became clear at this juncture that the stoker had seen a great deal of the world. With exemplary calm he took out of his small suitcase at the first attempt a small bundle of papers as well as a notebook, then as if it were a matter of course walked with these to the captain while completely ignoring the chief purser and spread his evidence out on the windowsill. The chief purser had no choice but to make his way there. “The man is a notorious complainer” he said by way of explanation “he’s in the purser’s office more than in the engine room. He has driven Schubal that calm person to complete despair.” “Now you listen!” he addressed the stoker “You’re really pushing your importunity too far. How many times have you been thrown out of the disbursement rooms by now, just as you deserve with your wholly, completely and invariably unjustified demands! How many times have you come running here to the main purser’s office from there! How many times have you been told kindly that Schubal is your immediate superior and it’s with him alone that you as his subordinate must come to terms! And now you even come here when the Herr Captain is present, not only aren’t ashamed to pester even him, but also have the effrontery to bring along as the trained spokesman of your tasteless accusations this little fellow, whom I’m seeing on the ship for the very first time.”

 Karl forcibly held himself back from jumping forward. But then the captain was already there, saying “Let’s just listen to the man. Lately Schubal has been growing far too independent for my liking anyhow, though by this I don’t mean to imply anything in your favor.” The last remark was directed at the stoker, it was only natural that he couldn’t take his side immediately, but everything seemed to be going in the right direction. The stoker began his explanations and at the very outset brought himself to refer to Schubal as Herr. How happy Karl was by the chief purser’s abandoned desk, where he kept pushing down a letter scale in sheer pleasure. Herr Schubal is unjust. Herr Schubal favors the foreigners. Herr Schubal banished the stoker from the engine room and made him clean toilets, which certainly wasn’t the stoker’s job. Once doubt was even cast on Herr Schubal’s capability, which was said to be more apparent than real. At this point Karl stared with all his might at the captain, trustingly as if he were his colleague, just so that he would not be influenced unfavorably by the stoker’s somewhat awkward manner of expression. All the same, one learned nothing substantial from his many speeches and even though the captain kept looking straight ahead, his eyes revealing his determination to hear the stoker out this time, the other gentlemen were growing impatient and the stoker’s voice soon no longer reigned absolutely in the room, which was cause for some fear. First the gentleman in civilian clothes set his bamboo cane in motion, tapping though only softly on the parquet floor. The other gentlemen naturally looked over now and then, the gentlemen from the harbor authority, apparently pressed for time, reached for their files again and began, even if still somewhat absentmindedly to look through them, the ship’s officer moved closer to his table again and the chief purser, believing he had won the day, heaved a deep ironic sigh. The only one who seemed unaffected by the distraction generally setting in was the servant, who sympathized to some extent with the woes of the poor man placed among the great ones and nodded gravely to Karl as if trying to explain something.

 Meanwhile the life of the harbor went on outside the windows, a flat cargo ship with a mountain of barrels, which must have been wonderfully stowed for them not to start rolling, passed by and plunged the room almost into darkness, small motor boats, which Karl could have examined closely now if he’d had time roared away in a straight line in accordance with the twitches of the hands of a man standing upright at the helm, peculiar floating objects emerged now and then of their own accord from the restless water, were immediately submerged again and sank before one’s astonished eyes, boats of the ocean steamers were rowed forward by furiously working sailors and were full of passengers who sat in them quietly and expectantly just as they had been squeezed in, even though some of them couldn’t refrain from turning their heads toward the changing scenery. A movement without end, a restlessness passed on from the restless element to the helpless people and their works.

 But everything urged haste, clarity, completely precise description, but what was the stoker doing? He was certainly talking himself into a sweat, he had long been unable to hold the papers on the window with his trembling hands, from all points of the compass complaints about Schubal streamed toward him, every single one of which in his opinion would have sufficed to bury that Schubal completely, but what he could show the captain was only a sad jumble of all of them whirled together. For a long time now the gentleman with the bamboo cane had been whistling faintly up at the ceiling, the gentlemen from the harbor authority had been holding the officer at their table and showed no sign of ever letting go of him again, the chief purser was visibly held back only by the captain’s calm from the intervention he was itching to make. The servant was standing at attention, waiting for his captain’s command regarding the stoker at any moment.

 At this point Karl could no longer remain idle. And so he walked slowly toward the group and while walking considered all the more quickly how he could tackle the matter as skillfully as possible. It was really high time, just a little while longer and both of them could quite easily be thrown out of the office. The captain certainly might be a good man and moreover might now of all times, as it seemed to Karl, have a special reason to show himself to be a just superior, but in the end he was not an instrument that one could play to its ruin—and that was exactly how the stoker was treating him, albeit out of his boundless inner indignation.

 And so Karl said to the stoker “You must tell the story more simply, more clearly, the Herr Captain can’t appreciate it the way you’re telling it to him. Is he supposed to know all the engineers and errand boys by name, or even by their Christian names, so that you need only utter one of these names for him to know immediately whom you mean. Organize your complaints, say the most important first and the others in descending order then perhaps it will no longer be necessary at all even to mention most of them. You’ve always described it to me so clearly.” If one can steal suitcases in America, one can also lie now and then, he thought by way of excuse.

 But if only it had helped! Might it not already be too late? The stoker did break off immediately when he heard the familiar voice, but with his eyes, which were completely obscured by tears, of offended manly honor, of terrible memories, of extreme present distress, he could hardly even recognize Karl anymore. How should he now, Karl came to this realization silently before the now silent man, how should he now suddenly change his manner of speaking, when it seemed to him, after all, as if he had already brought up everything there was to say without the slightest acknowledgment and on the other hand as if he had said nothing at all yet and certainly couldn’t now expect the gentlemen to listen to everything from the beginning. And at a moment like this Karl his sole supporter comes along, wanting to give him good advice, but instead shows him that all all is lost.

 If only I had come sooner instead of looking out the window Karl said to himself, lowering his face before the stoker and slapping his hands against the seams of his pants as a sign of the end of all hope.

 But the stoker misunderstood this, probably scenting in Karl some secret reproaches against him and with the good intention of talking him out of them he now crowned his deeds by beginning to quarrel with Karl. Now, when the gentlemen at the round table had long since grown indignant at the useless noise disturbing their important work, when the chief purser was gradually finding the captain’s patience incomprehensible and was on the verge of an outburst, when the servant once again completely in the sphere of his masters was measuring the stoker with a wild look and when finally the gentleman with the bamboo cane, at whom even the captain occasionally cast a friendly glance having become entirely deadened to the stoker indeed disgusted by him, pulled out a small notebook and evidently preoccupied with completely different matters let his eyes wander back and forth between the notebook and Karl.

 Yes I know, I know said Karl who was having difficulty fending off the torrent the stoker was now directing at him, but through all the quarreling could still spare a friendly smile for him. You’re right, you’re right, I’ve never once doubted it. He would have liked to hold his gesticulating hands for fear of being struck, though he would have liked even more to drive him into a corner and whisper a few soft soothing words to him, which no one else would have to hear. But the stoker was out of control. Karl now even began to draw a sort of consolation from the thought that if need be the stoker could vanquish all seven men present with the strength of his despair. To be sure, the desk as a glance in that direction revealed had an upper part with far too many push buttons of the electric line, and one hand, simply pressed down on them, could incite the whole ship with all its corridors filled with hostile people to rebellion.

 Now the gentleman with the bamboo cane, who had been so uninterested, approached Karl and asked not too loudly, but clearly over all the stoker’s shouting: What’s your name anyway? At that moment, as if someone behind the door had been waiting for the gentleman to say these words there was a knock. The servant looked over at the captain, the latter nodded. And so the servant went to the door and opened it. Outside stood a man of medium proportions in an old frock coat, judging by his appearance not actually suited to work on the engines and yet he was—Schubal. If Karl hadn’t recognized this in everyone’s eyes, which expressed a certain satisfaction, of which not even the captain was free, he would inevitably have seen it, to his horror, in the stoker, who clenched his fists on his tensed arms as if this clenching were the most important thing about him, for which he was prepared to sacrifice all the life he had. All his strength was now concentrated there, even that which held him upright.

 And so here was the enemy free and fresh in festive attire, under his arm an account book, probably the stoker’s wage lists and work records and, boldly admitting that he wanted above all to determine the mood of every single one of them he looked into everyone’s eyes in turn. All seven were already his friends anyhow, for even if the captain had previously had or perhaps only feigned certain objections to him, after the suffering the stoker had inflicted on him, he could probably no longer find even the slightest fault with Schubal. Against a man like the stoker one couldn’t proceed severely enough and if
